title: Sizing the Vocabulary
concept: LLM tokenizers use large vocabularies (e.g. 32k to 256k tokens) to represent common words as single tokens, saving context window space.

exercises.prompt: Before we talk about vocabulary size, what are 'tokens' in the context of LLMs?

options.text: Chunks of text like words or sub-words
The physical cables inside a server

exercises.explain: Tokens are the fundamental building blocks (like words or parts of words) that language models use to read and process text.

cards.title: Reading in Chunks
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how AI reads so fast? It doesn't read letter by letter like we do when we are first learning to read. Instead, it groups characters into chunks called tokens. By using a massive 'vocabulary' of these chunks, the AI can read whole words at a single glance!

cards.title: What is Vocabulary Size?
cards.body: Vocabulary size is the total number of unique pieces (tokens)—like individual characters, word parts, and whole words—that an LLM's tokenizer is trained to recognize. Modern LLMs typically use vocabularies containing between 32,000 and 256,000 distinct tokens.

cards.title: The Stamp Set Analogy
cards.body: Imagine writing a letter using a stamp set. If you only have stamps for single letters (A, B, C), it takes forever to stamp out a sentence. But if you have stamps for whole common words like 'the', 'and', or 'hello', you can stamp your message much faster using far fewer stamp marks! A large vocabulary gives an LLM these pre-made word stamps.

cards.title: Tokens in Action
cards.body:
Consider the phrase 'learning is fun'. 

• With a small vocabulary, a tokenizer might split this into 5 tokens: ['learn', 'ing', ' is', ' f', 'un'].
• With a large vocabulary, it recognizes these words easily and splits it into just 3 tokens: ['learning', ' is', ' fun']. 

This saves two valuable slots in the AI's limited memory window!

cards.title: Is Bigger Always Better?
cards.body: It's easy to think that a larger vocabulary always makes an LLM smarter. However, a huge vocabulary makes the AI's 'brain' heavier because it has to remember and choose from more options. AI creators must find the perfect balance between vocabulary size and speed!

cards.title: Visualizing Vocab Size
cards.body: Let's compare how a small vocabulary and a large vocabulary process the exact same sentence. Notice how the large vocabulary packages the text into fewer total chunks.

exercises.prompt: What is a typical vocabulary size range for modern Large Language Models?
exercises.explain: A vocabulary size of 32,000 to 256,000 tokens allows the AI to represent many common words as single tokens, saving computational resources and space.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Using a larger vocabulary size generally decreases the total number of tokens needed to represent a piece of text.
exercises.explain: A larger vocabulary allows the tokenizer to bundle letters into larger, single tokens (like whole words), which reduces the total number of tokens needed to represent a paragraph.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the rule about vocabulary sizing:
exercises.sentence: A larger vocabulary allows the AI to represent the same text using _ tokens.
exercises.explain: A larger vocabulary allows the tokenizer to find larger matches for common words, which results in fewer total tokens being used for the text.

exercises.prompt: Two of these statements explain vocabulary sizing correctly, but one is an AI hallucination. Identify the incorrect AI statement:

options.text: By using a vocabulary of 100,000 tokens, the tokenizer can represent whole words as single tokens, keeping the total token count low.
A large vocabulary means the model can process long books using fewer tokens, preserving the limits of its memory window.
If we use a very small vocabulary of just single letters, we save space because individual letters are tiny to store.

exercises.explain: The third option is generated by an AI because it incorrectly states that a small vocabulary of individual letters saves space. In reality, spelling everything out letter-by-letter takes up much more space in the context window.

exercises.prompt: How does a larger vocabulary size (like 100k+ tokens) help save space in an LLM's context window?

options.text: It makes the context window physically larger.
It packs more meaning into fewer tokens, leaving more free space in the window.
It forces the AI to use simple words to save space.
It deletes uncommon words from the user's prompt.

exercises.explain: A larger vocabulary size means more common words are grouped into single tokens. This reduces the total token count for a text, leaving more room in the LLM's limited context window for longer conversations or documents.
